1: A revelation of God: “Is it not for you to know justice?” — the leaders of Israel are accountable for justice precisely because they were given authority to administer it. The God who holds them accountable is the God whose nature is justice (Psalms 89:14: “righteousness and justice are the foundation of your throne”). Christ's woes against the scribes and Pharisees (Matt 23) are the NT form of Micah's challenge to the rulers: those entrusted with the law bear the heaviest accountability for its violation. The justice YHWH requires is the justice Christ both embodies and demands.
2: A shadow: “You who hate the good and love evil, who tear the skin from my people.” The inversion of the created moral order — hating good, loving evil — is the diagnostic of deep corruption. Paul uses the same categories: “Although they know God's righteous decree that those who do such things deserve death, they not only continue to do these very things but also approve of those who practice them” (Romans 1:32). Christ's coming as the “good and true” one (John 10:11, 14) into a world that hates the good explains the hostility he encounters: “the light has come into the world, and people loved the darkness rather than the light” (John 3:19).
3: A shadow: the cannibalistic image — rulers who “eat the flesh of my people, break their bones in pieces, chop them up like meat for the pot” — is the OT's most visceral description of exploitative leadership. Shepherds who devour the flock rather than protect it (Ezek 34 develops the same image at length). Christ as the Good Shepherd is the antithesis: he lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11) instead of consuming them. The flesh that the corrupt shepherds devour from the people, Christ gives his own flesh for: “my flesh is true food” (John 6:55) — the eucharistic reversal of the cannibalistic exploitation Micah exposes.
4: A shadow: “Then they will cry out to the LORD, but he will not answer them; he will hide his face from them at that time.” The hidden face of YHWH as divine judgment — withdrawal of the presence that had sustained them. The most devastating form of judgment is not external punishment but divine absence. Christ's cry from the cross — “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” (Matthew 27:46) — is the ultimate hidden face. The Son endures the hidden face not for his own evil deeds (unlike these rulers) but for ours, so that those who repent will never experience YHWH's permanent withdrawal: “I will never leave you nor forsake you” (Hebrews 13:5).
5: A shadow: the false prophets who “cry peace when they have something to eat, but declare war against him who puts nothing into their mouths” — their prophecies are market-driven, not Spirit-driven. The one true prophet who is not for sale is the contrast: Christ “taught them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes” (Matthew 7:29). The false prophet speaks what the audience pays for; Christ speaks the truth regardless of cost (John 7:7: “the world cannot hate you, but it hates me because I testify about it that its works are evil”). The true prophet who speaks without payment becomes the one who is condemned by those who pay for comfortable prophecy.
6: A shadow: the judgment on false prophets — vision becomes darkness, divination becomes night, the sun goes down over them — is the reversal of prophetic light into blindness. The Day of the LORD as darkness for those who live by false light. Christ is the reversal: “I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness” (John 8:12). The sun that sets on false prophets is the same sun that rises on those who fear YHWH's name: “the sun of righteousness shall rise with healing in its wings” (Malachi 4:2). Christ is the light that the darkness of false prophecy cannot extinguish (John 1:5).
7: A theme: the seers covered their lips, the diviners disgraced — shame and silence when the word of God is withdrawn. The mouth covered was the gesture of ritual uncleanness (Leviticus 13:45: the leper covered his lip crying “Unclean!”). The false prophets' silence exposes them as the spiritual lepers they always were. The contrast is Christ, who never covers his lips, never loses the prophetic word, because he is himself the Word: the prophet greater than Moses who speaks with God face to face (Deuteronomy 18:15-18; John 1:1).
8: A theme: “But as for me, I am filled with power, with the Spirit of the LORD, and with justice and might, to declare to Jacob his transgression and to Israel his sin.” Micah's prophetic empowerment — the Spirit, justice, and might — contrasts directly with the false prophets' market-driven darkness (v6-7). The Spirit's empowerment for truthful witness is the OT pattern that reaches its fulfillment in Christ: “the Spirit of the LORD is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor” (Luke 4:18, citing Isaiah 61:1). The same Spirit that empowered Micah rests on Christ “without measure” (John 3:34).
9: A revelation of God: “Hear this, you heads of the house of Jacob and rulers of the house of Israel, who detest justice and make crooked all that is straight.” YHWH holds covenant leadership to the highest standard of justice; those who make straight things crooked are accountable to the one who is himself straight/upright (yashar). Christ “makes straight” all that is crooked — the one who fulfills the whole law and cannot be accused of making it crooked (John 8:46: “which one of you convicts me of sin?”). The very sin Micah condemns in Israel's rulers, Christ is entirely free of.
10: A shadow: “who build Zion with blood and Jerusalem with iniquity.” The city built on violent exploitation — a Zion constructed through injustice — cannot stand. The NT identifies a greater and permanent Zion: “you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem” (Hebrews 12:22). This city is not built with blood shed by its inhabitants' exploitation but with the blood of Christ who builds it (Matthew 16:18: “I will build my church”). The corrupted earthly Zion is replaced by the indestructible heavenly city founded on the one righteous foundation.
11: A shadow: “Its heads give judgment for a bribe; its priests teach for a price; its prophets practice divination for money.” The complete commercialization of covenant service — every mediator between YHWH and the people operates for hire. The contrast is Christ, who gives his life without charge: “You received without paying; give without pay” (Matthew 10:8); “Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters; and he who has no money, come” (Isaiah 55:1). The mediator who charges nothing for access to God is the answer to every corrupt mediator who charges everything.
12: A shadow: “Therefore because of you Zion shall be plowed as a field, and Jerusalem shall become a heap of ruins.” The most shocking oracle in Micah — the holy city will be destroyed, plowed under like a common field. This was cited a century later (Jeremiah 26:18-19) as the precedent for not killing Jeremiah. The destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BCE partially fulfilled it; the Roman destruction of 70 CE completed it (Luke 21:6: “not one stone will be left here upon another that will not be thrown down” — Christ's own prophecy echoing Micah). The plowing of the city is the judgment that the cross both absorbs and delays: Christ bears the covenant curse so the city has another generation to repent.
